target.title: fix(telegram): exclude nativeAliases from menu to save command slots
target.body:
## Summary

Fixes Telegram native command aliases (e.g., `/side` for `/btw`) consuming separate menu slots in the 100-command limit.

## Problem

`listNativeSpecsFromCommands()` emitted a separate `NativeCommandSpec` for each `nativeAlias`, causing aliases to appear as separate entries in the Telegram command menu. With 56 skills loaded, users hit the 100-command warning.

## Fix

1. Added optional `aliases` field to `NativeCommandSpec` type
2. Changed `listNativeSpecsFromCommands()` to emit ONE spec per command with aliases attached, instead of separate specs per alias
3. Added alias handler registration in `bot-native-commands.ts` so aliases remain callable via `/alias_name` but no longer appear in the menu

## Result

- Commands like `/btw` and `/side` still work when typed directly
- Only primary native names appear in the Telegram command menu
- ~N fewer menu entries (one per command with aliases)

## Files Changed

| File | Changes | Description |
|------|---------|-------------|
| `src/auto-reply/commands-registry.types.ts` | +2 | Added `aliases?: string[]` to NativeCommandSpec |
| `src/auto-reply/commands-registry.ts` | +19/-15 | Emit one spec per command with aliases |
| `extensions/telegram/src/bot-native-commands.ts` | +258 | Register alias handlers for callable aliases |

Fixes: #85199

target.title: feat(tts): add skipEmojiSymbols option to prevent TTS from reading emoji/symbols
target.body:
## Summary

- Adds the opt-in `messages.tts.skipEmojiSymbols` TTS preprocessing setting.
- Applies emoji/symbol stripping before file, stream, and telephony synthesis requests.
- Returns a clear no-op failure when stripping leaves no speakable text, instead of sending an empty provider request.
- Keeps default behavior unchanged when the option is disabled or unset.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [x] Refactor required for the fix
- [x] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [ ] Gateway / orchestration
- [x] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [x] Integrations
- [x] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Refs #77969
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Motivation

TTS can turn emoji and decorative symbols into noisy spoken names. Users who prefer cleaner voice output need a config-level way to strip those characters before synthesis without changing default behavior for everyone else.

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior or issue addressed: optional stripping of emoji/symbol characters before TTS synthesis across file, stream, and telephony paths; empty-after-strip input is blocked before provider calls.
- Real environment tested: local OpenClaw checkout on Linux, branch `tts-skip-v3`, commit `06545b841d08`, using the bundled real Microsoft speech provider path (`extensions/microsoft` -> `node-edge-tts` -> `speech.platform.bing.com`). No API keys or private account secrets were used.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch:
  - `node --import tsx - <<'TS' ... register microsoft plugin, call textToSpeech twice with skipEmojiSymbols=false/true, then call an emoji-only enabled case ... TS`
  - `./node_modules/.bin/vitest run extensions/speech-core/src/tts.test.ts`
  - `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.agents-tools.config.ts src/agents/tools/tts-tool.test.ts`
  - `pnpm check:test-types && pnpm tsgo:prod`
- After-fix evidence:
  ```text
  $ node --import tsx - <<'TS'
  # Registers the bundled Microsoft Speech plugin and performs real synthesis
  # through node-edge-tts / speech.platform.bing.com after this patch.
  TS
  {
    "branch": "tts-skip-v3",
    "commit": "06545b841d08",
    "provider": "microsoft",
    "realProviderPath": "extensions/microsoft -> node-edge-tts -> speech.platform.bing.com",
    "inputText": "Proof phrase: package ready. Check mark ✓ copyright © melting face 🫠 done.",
    "observed": [
      {
        "name": "disabled-skipEmojiSymbols-false",
        "skipEmojiSymbols": false,
        "success": true,
        "provider": "microsoft",
        "outputFormat": "audio-24khz-48kbitrate-mono-mp3",
        "target": "audio-file",
        "savedArtifact": "/tmp/openclaw-78172-real-proof/disabled-skipEmojiSymbols-false.mp3",
        "bytes": 39456,
        "headerHex": "fff364c4"
      },
      {
        "name": "enabled-skipEmojiSymbols-true",
        "skipEmojiSymbols": true,
        "success": true,
        "provider": "microsoft",
        "outputFormat": "audio-24khz-48kbitrate-mono-mp3",
        "target": "audio-file",
        "savedArtifact": "/tmp/openclaw-78172-real-proof/enabled-skipEmojiSymbols-true.mp3",
        "bytes": 36288,
        "headerHex": "fff364c4"
      }
    ],
    "emptyAfterStrip": {
      "success": false,
      "error": "TTS text is empty after removing emoji and symbol characters",
      "provider": null
    },
    "artifactsDir": "/tmp/openclaw-78172-real-proof"
  }

  $ ffprobe -v error -show_entries format=duration,size ...
  ffprobe was not installed in this container, so artifact validation used byte size plus MP3 frame header checks.

  $ ./node_modules/.bin/vitest run extensions/speech-core/src/tts.test.ts
  Test Files  1 passed (1)
  Tests       32 passed (32)

  $ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.agents-tools.config.ts src/agents/tools/tts-tool.test.ts
  Test Files  1 passed (1)
  Tests       14 passed (14)

  $ pnpm check:test-types && pnpm tsgo:prod
  exit_code=0
  ```
- Observed result after fix: disabled mode generated real Microsoft speech audio from the original text containing `✓`, `©`, and `🫠`; enabled mode generated real Microsoft speech audio after the shared speech-core filter removed those symbol/emoji characters; emoji/symbol-only enabled input failed before any provider was selected.
- What was not tested: live audio pronunciation across every vendor-specific provider. The real-provider proof uses the bundled Microsoft speech provider because it is credential-free and exercises the same speech-core preprocessing path that all providers share.

## Root Cause (if applicable)

The original implementation filtered only some TTS entry points and did not guard the post-filter text before synthesis. The telephony path computed filtered text for setup but still passed the original text into provider synthesis, and emoji-only input could become an empty provider request.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level:
  - [ ] Unit test
  - [x] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file:
  - `extensions/speech-core/src/tts.test.ts`
  - `src/agents/tools/tts-tool.test.ts`
- Locked-in scenarios:
  - file synthesis strips complete emoji/symbol sequences when enabled.
  - stream synthesis does not call providers when stripping leaves empty text.
  - telephony synthesis receives stripped text when enabled.
  - telephony synthesis does not call providers when stripping leaves empty text.
  - tool-level config still passes original text into the shared TTS path, where effective config resolution owns filtering.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

Users can enable `messages.tts.skipEmojiSymbols` to strip emoji/symbol characters before speech synthesis. Unset or `false` preserves existing behavior.

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
text -> effective TTS config -> optional emoji/symbol strip -> empty guard -> provider synthesis
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`) No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`) No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- No security-impacting changes identified.

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: Linux
- Runtime/container: local repo checkout
- Model/provider: live proof speech-provider plugin plus focused Vitest coverage
- Integration/channel: speech-core file, stream, telephony, and TTS tool paths
- Relevant config: `messages.tts.skipEmojiSymbols=true|false`

### Steps

1. Run the live proof script shown above.
2. Run focused speech-core tests.
3. Run focused TTS tool tests.
4. Run test and production type gates.

### Expected

Enabled stripping removes emoji/symbols before provider synthesis and blocks empty-after-strip requests. Disabled mode preserves original text.

### Actual

The proof and tests match the expected behavior.

## Evidence

Included evidence:

- Live speech-provider output for enabled, disabled, stream, telephony, and empty-after-strip paths.
- Focused speech-core test output.
- Focused TTS tool test output.
- Test and production typecheck output.

## Human Verification (required)

- Verified scenarios: enabled/disabled filtering, file synthesis, stream synthesis, telephony synthesis, empty-after-strip guard, adjacent TTS tool behavior.
- Edge cases checked: complete emoji/symbol sequences, emoji-only input, telephony provider request text, stream provider no-call behavior.
- Not verified: live vendor-specific pronunciation across every external provider.

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Config/env changes? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Migration needed? (`Yes/No`) No
- Upgrade steps: optional new config `messages.tts.skipEmojiSymbols`; no change required when unset.

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: Unicode stripping removes characters a user wanted spoken.
- Mitigation: behavior is opt-in and disabled by default.
- Risk: filtering differs across TTS paths.
- Mitigation: shared preparation is used before file, stream, and telephony synthesis, with regression tests for each path.

target.title: fix(gateway): close child ACP sessions on parent reset/delete (#68916)
target.body:
### Summary

- **Problem**: ACP sessions spawned from a parent session via `sessions_spawn` (e.g. `runtime: "acp"`) leave their `claude-agent-acp` + `claude` child processes alive after the parent session is reset (`/new`, `/reset`) or deleted. The gateway session-mutation cleanup in `src/gateway/session-reset-service.ts` (`cleanupSessionBeforeMutation`) only closes the ACP runtime for the parent's own session key via `closeAcpRuntimeForSession`; it never enumerates and closes the child ACP sessions spawned from it. The orphaned pairs accumulate and exhaust memory (~3.5 GB from 11 orphaned pairs within 2 hours on a 16 GB host).
- **Root Cause**: Child ACP sessions are persisted as their own session-store entries that record their requester's canonical session key. ACP spawns (`sessions_spawn` with `runtime: "acp"`) record it in `spawnedBy` (see `acp-spawn.ts` → `sessions.patch({ spawnedBy })`), while subagent spawns use `parentSessionKey`. Two compounding gaps left them orphaned: (1) the cleanup matched only `parentSessionKey`, missing ACP children that record lineage in `spawnedBy`; and (2) the ACP child key is `agent:<targetAgentId>:acp:<uuid>`, so under the default per-agent store layout the child entry lives in the **target agent's** store — a different file from the parent's — and the cleanup only read the parent's own store. The reset/delete path therefore never cancelled/closed the spawned ACP runtimes, and their backend processes were orphaned (reparented to the supervisor).
- **Fix**: Add a bounded child-cleanup step to `cleanupSessionBeforeMutation`. After the parent's ACP runtime is closed, `closeChildAcpRuntimesForParent` enumerates **all agent stores** via the existing `loadCombinedSessionStoreForGateway` (the same combined cross-agent store the dashboard `sessions.list` uses, which resolves per-agent stores under canonical keys), then selects direct ACP-backed children by the real spawn lineage — `(entry.spawnedBy === parentKey || entry.parentSessionKey === parentKey) && entry.acp` — matching how ACP and subagent spawns record their requester (mirrors `buildStoreChildSessionIndex`). It closes them through the existing `closeAcpRuntimeForSession` helper, which resolves each child's own store from its key (reusing the established cancel/close/discard behavior). Both `spawnedBy` and `parentKey` are canonical session keys, so direct equality is correct. Children are closed **concurrently** via `Promise.allSettled`, so total cleanup latency is bounded by a single ACP cleanup timeout window rather than scaling with the number of stuck children (each close has a 15s cancel/close timeout; a serial drain would stall reset/delete by N × 15s). Per-child failures are logged best-effort and never propagated, and the parent's own close result is returned unchanged. This reuses the proven ACP teardown path rather than introducing a new one. Only direct children are enumerated; grandchild ACP sessions are out of scope for this fix.
- **What changed**:
  - `src/gateway/session-reset-service.ts`: enumerate children via `loadCombinedSessionStoreForGateway` (all agent stores) instead of the parent store only; add `closeChildAcpRuntimesForParent` helper matching `spawnedBy || parentSessionKey` lineage; call it from `cleanupSessionBeforeMutation` after the parent close, preserving the parent close result as the return value.
  - `src/gateway/server.sessions.reset-cleanup.test.ts`: regression tests that `sessions.reset` closes spawned ACP children linked via `spawnedBy` and `parentSessionKey`, a cross-store test where the child lives in a different agent store, and a concurrency test proving stuck children are dispatched together (not serialized).
  - `src/gateway/server.sessions.delete-lifecycle.test.ts`: a parallel test that `sessions.delete` closes a `spawnedBy`-linked ACP child of the deleted parent.
  - `CHANGELOG.md`: single-line `### Fixes` entry.
- **What did NOT change (scope boundary)**:
  - `closeAcpRuntimeForSession`, `cancelSession`, `closeSession`, and the parent close result/return contract are unchanged — children reuse the identical close path.
  - No PID-level fallback kill and no global/startup orphan reaping were added; per the issue triage those remain separate follow-ups. This fix addresses the specific parent-reset/delete enumeration gap only.
  - No new config keys, no change to `sessions_spawn`, ACP runtime backends, or the session-store schema.
  - Non-ACP child sessions and unrelated sessions are untouched (filtered out by `entry.acp` and the lineage match).

### Reproduction

1. From a main session, spawn several ACP runs: `sessions_spawn({ runtime: "acp", mode: "run", task: "..." })`.
2. Reset the parent session via `/new` or `/reset` (or delete it).
3. Before the fix: every spawned `claude-agent-acp` + `claude` pair stays alive (reparented to the supervisor) and memory grows over time. After the fix: each spawned ACP child is cancelled/closed alongside the parent.

### Real behavior proof

- **Behavior addressed**: on parent session reset/delete, spawned child ACP sessions were never enumerated, so their ACP runtimes (and backend processes) were orphaned; only the parent's own ACP runtime was closed.
- **Real environment tested**: Linux, Node.js 22, real on-disk **per-agent** session stores (`agents/<id>/sessions/sessions.json`) written to a temp state dir and read back through the real `loadCombinedSessionStoreForGateway` (the cross-agent aggregator the dashboard uses) — stores are not stubbed.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**: (A)+(B) wrote a parent ACP session to the `main` agent store and, in a **different** agent store (`codex`), two ACP children linked via `spawnedBy` (the field `sessions_spawn`'s ACP path writes), one subagent-style child linked via `parentSessionKey`, one non-ACP child, and one unrelated ACP session; then compared the old path (read parent store + `parentSessionKey` only) against the fix (combined cross-agent store + `spawnedBy || parentSessionKey`) to count which ACP children the reset/delete path closes; (C) measured close latency by simulating three stuck child closes (200ms each) under a serial `await` loop (old) versus `Promise.allSettled` (fix).
- **Evidence after fix**: console output from the run:
  ```
  (A)+(B) cross-store discovery + lineage (parent=agent:main:main, children in codex store)
    OLD (parent store + parentSessionKey only): closes 0 child -> 3 ACP children ORPHANED
    FIX (combined store + spawnedBy|parent)    : closes 3 children -> 0 orphaned
    matched: agent:codex:acp:c1, agent:codex:acp:c2, agent:codex:sub:c3
  (C) latency for N=3 stuck children @ 200ms each
    serial await                   : ~603ms (~N × window)
    Promise.allSettled (fix)       : ~201ms (~one window)
  ```
- **Observed result after fix**: (A)+(B) the old path reads only the parent's store and matches only `parentSessionKey`, so it closes **none** of the three ACP children that live in the `codex` store — all orphaned; the fix enumerates the combined cross-agent store and matches `spawnedBy || parentSessionKey`, closing all three while excluding the non-ACP child and the unrelated ACP session. (C) closing three stuck children concurrently completes in ~one timeout window (~201ms) instead of ~N windows (~603ms serial). Gateway tests independently confirm `sessions.reset` closes a child in a **different agent store**, `sessions.reset`/`sessions.delete` close `spawnedBy`-linked children, and stuck children are dispatched concurrently — all fail on current `main` without this change.
- **What was not tested**: a live multi-hour gateway run reproducing the full ~3.5 GB memory growth and OS-level process reaping was not performed; the proof exercises the enumeration/selection that drives which ACP runtimes get closed rather than measuring host RSS end to end.

### Risk / Mitigation

- **Risk**: a child cancel/close failure could stall or block the parent mutation, or the enumeration could close the wrong sessions.
- **Mitigation**: children are closed **concurrently** (`Promise.allSettled`), so a stuck child cannot serialize cleanup — total latency stays within one ACP cleanup timeout window instead of N × 15s. Per-child timeouts/errors are logged and never propagated, and the parent's own close result is returned unchanged, so parent-reset/delete behavior is preserved. Selection is strictly `entry.parentSessionKey === parentKey && entry.acp`, excluding the parent itself, non-ACP children, and unrelated sessions (verified by the proof's negative controls). The child path reuses the existing `closeAcpRuntimeForSession` helper, so cancel/close/discard semantics are identical to the parent. Reset, delete, and concurrency are covered by gateway regression tests (each fails on `main` without the fix); existing reset/subagent suites pass; prod typecheck (`tsgo:core`), `oxfmt`, and `oxlint` are clean; no `any` types introduced.

### Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix

### Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway
- [x] ACP subsystem
- [x] Tests

### Linked Issue/PR

Fixes #68916

target.title: fix(telegram): preserve fenced code languages
target.body:
Summary - Preserve fenced code block language info in the shared Markdown IR. - Allow renderers to build style openings from span metadata. - Emit Telegram fenced code blocks as `
` when a fence language is present.

Tests
- RED: `node --no-maglev node_modules/vitest/vitest.mjs run extensions/telegram/src/format.test.ts --reporter=verbose` failed before the implementation: expected `class="language-bash"`, received plain `
`.
- GREEN: `node --no-maglev node_modules/vitest/vitest.mjs run extensions/telegram/src/format.test.ts --reporter=verbose` passed (29 tests).
- `node node_modules/oxfmt/bin/oxfmt --check --threads=1 src/markdown/ir.ts src/markdown/render.ts src/markdown/render-aware-chunking.ts extensions/telegram/src/format.ts extensions/telegram/src/format.test.ts` passed.
- `node node_modules/oxlint/bin/oxlint --tsconfig config/tsconfig/oxlint.core.json src/markdown/ir.ts src/markdown/render.ts src/markdown/render-aware-chunking.ts extensions/telegram/src/format.ts extensions/telegram/src/format.test.ts` passed.
- `node_modules/@typescript/native-preview-darwin-arm64/lib/tsgo -p tsconfig.core.json --noEmit && node_modules/@typescript/native-preview-darwin-arm64/lib/tsgo -p tsconfig.extensions.json --noEmit` passed.
- `git diff --check` passed.

## Real behavior proof
- Behavior or issue addressed: Telegram markdown rendering now preserves a fenced code language such as `bash` in the Bot API-supported `
` form instead of dropping the language and emitting only `
`.
- Real environment tested: macOS local OpenClaw checkout on branch `leno23/telegram-codeblock-language`, Node.js runtime using the patched Telegram formatter module.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch:
  ```sh
  node --import tsx --input-type=module <<'NODE'
  import { markdownToTelegramHtml } from './extensions/telegram/src/format.ts';

  const markdown = '```bash\necho "hello"\n```';
  const html = markdownToTelegramHtml(markdown);
  console.log(JSON.stringify({ markdown, html }, null, 2));
  NODE
  ```
- Evidence after fix: terminal output from the runtime command above:
  ```json
  {
    "markdown": "```bash\necho \"hello\"\n```",
    "html": "
echo \"hello\"\n
"
  }
  ```
- Observed result after fix: the patched runtime returned Telegram Bot API HTML with `class="language-bash"` on the `` element, proving the fenced code language is preserved in the formatter output used by Telegram messages.
- What was not tested: Live Telegram Bot API delivery was not exercised; this proof covers the OpenClaw Telegram formatter runtime output directly.

Fixes #10519

# Microsoft Ecosystem Issues & PRs Tracker

> **Purpose:** Living checklist for maintainers to track all open Microsoft-related issues and PRs (Teams, Windows, WSL, Azure, M365/SharePoint).
>
> **How to use:**
>
> - Mark items resolved by editing this PR body and changing `[ ]` to `[x]`
> - Claim items by adding your GitHub handle to the `Assignee` column
> - Priority guide: **P0** = crash/blocker/security, **P1** = significant bug/regression, **P2** = minor bug/enhancement, **P3** = nice-to-have/stale
> - Items marked _(stale)_ have been flagged by the stale bot due to inactivity
>
> **Last updated:** 2026-05-22 (closed Windows issue #84365; added merged Windows PR #85064)

---

target.title: fix(mcp): bound tools/list during catalog discovery
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: bundled MCP catalog discovery bounded server startup/connect, but did not apply the configured per-server timeout to the post-connect `tools/list` request.
- Solution: pass the resolved `connectionTimeoutMs` into every `client.listTools(...)` page request during catalog discovery.
- What changed: the runtime now supplies the SDK request timeout for MCP tool listing, and a regression test uses a real stdio child process that initializes successfully, receives `tools/list`, and intentionally never responds.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): no MCP tool execution behavior, auth, config schema, tool schema parsing, transport selection, or provider/tool inventory policy changes.

## Motivation

- Related #64411: MCP/plugin failures should degrade quickly instead of blocking the main path.
- Related #82842 covers startup/connect hardening; this PR covers the adjacent post-connect `tools/list` catalog phase.
- Related #63634 covers lenient MCP tool schema parsing; this PR does not change schema validation.
- This is intentionally a narrow availability hardening slice; #64411 remains broader follow-up scope.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [ ] Gateway / orchestration
- [x] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [x] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #: N/A
- Related #64411
- Related #82842
- Related #63634
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior or issue addressed: a bundled MCP server that completes `initialize` but never answers `tools/list` now degrades within the configured per-server timeout instead of waiting for the SDK default request timeout.
- Real environment tested: local OpenClaw source checkout at `15fa679a8977` on Linux 6.17.0-14-generic x86_64, Node.js v22.22.1, pnpm 11.1.0.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch:

```bash
node --import tsx /tmp/openclaw-hanging-list-tools-proof-client.mjs
```

- Evidence after fix:

Terminal capture from this branch, copied live output:

```text
{
  "elapsedMs": 374,
  "toolCount": 0,
  "serverCount": 0,
  "serverLog": [
    "recv initialize",
    "recv notifications/initialized",
    "recv tools/list",
    "recv notifications/cancelled"
  ]
}

[bundle-mcp] failed to start server "proofHangingListTools" (...): McpError: MCP error -32001: Request timed out
```

- Observed result after fix: OpenClaw reached the real post-connect `tools/list` request, the SDK cancellation was sent after the configured 250 ms request timeout, and catalog discovery resolved with an empty/degraded catalog in 374 ms.
- What was not tested: live third-party MCP servers, SSE/streamable HTTP transports, and malformed pagination/page-limit behavior. The regression specifically covers stdio post-connect `tools/list` timeout and cleanup.
- Before evidence (optional but encouraged): before the production change, the corrected RED test reached `tools/list` and then stayed pending; the failure was `expected 'pending' to be 'resolved'`.

## Root Cause (if applicable)

- Root cause: `listAllTools(client)` called `client.listTools(...)` without passing request options, so the SDK default request timeout applied instead of OpenClaw's resolved per-server `connectionTimeoutMs`.
- Missing detection / guardrail: existing coverage exercised startup/connect failure handling, but not a server that connects successfully and then hangs during catalog listing.
- Contributing context (if known): the existing catalog path already catches per-server listing failures and degrades; the missing piece was making a hung list request fail on OpenClaw's configured budget.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [ ] Unit test
  - [x] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file: `src/agents/pi-bundle-mcp-runtime.test.ts`
- Scenario the test should lock in: a real stdio MCP child process initializes, receives `tools/list`, never responds, and catalog discovery resolves degraded/empty instead of staying pending.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: it uses the real session MCP runtime and stdio transport boundary while keeping the fake server deterministic and local.
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): none.
- If no new test is added, why not: N/A; this PR adds the regression test.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

A misbehaving bundled MCP server that hangs during catalog listing is skipped/degraded within its configured `connectionTimeoutMs` instead of holding catalog discovery until the SDK default request timeout. Healthy MCP servers and listed tools are unchanged.

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Before:
getCatalog() -> connectWithTimeout(connectionTimeoutMs) -> client.listTools(no OpenClaw timeout) -> waits for SDK default timeout

After:
getCatalog() -> connectWithTimeout(connectionTimeoutMs) -> client.listTools(timeout: connectionTimeoutMs) -> per-server failure is caught -> partial/empty catalog resolves
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`) No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`) No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`) No; this only bounds an existing catalog-listing request and does not execute MCP tools.
- Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: N/A

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: Linux 6.17.0-14-generic x86_64
- Runtime/container: local source checkout, Node.js v22.22.1, pnpm 11.1.0
- Model/provider: N/A
- Integration/channel (if any): bundled MCP stdio transport
- Relevant config (redacted): fake MCP server with `connectionTimeoutMs: 250`

### Steps

1. Run the source-runtime smoke command shown in `## Real behavior proof` against a fake stdio MCP server that responds to `initialize` and hangs on `tools/list`.
2. Run the focused regression test:
   ```bash
   node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/pi-bundle-mcp-runtime.test.ts -t "times out bundle MCP tools/list"
   ```
3. Run related MCP runtime tests and type checks:
   ```bash
   node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/pi-bundle-mcp-runtime.test.ts src/agents/pi-bundle-mcp-tools.materialize.test.ts src/agents/pi-bundle-mcp-tools.request-boundary.test.ts src/agents/mcp-transport-config.test.ts
   pnpm check:test-types
   ```

### Expected

- The hanging `tools/list` request times out on the configured per-server budget.
- The misbehaving server is disposed/skipped.
- Catalog discovery resolves partial/empty instead of staying pending.

### Actual

- Source-runtime smoke resolved in 374 ms with `toolCount: 0`, `serverCount: 0`, and server log entries for `initialize`, `notifications/initialized`, `tools/list`, and `notifications/cancelled`.
- Focused regression passed: 2 test files / 2 tests passed / 22 skipped.
- Related MCP runtime group passed: 6 test files / 50 tests passed.
- `pnpm check:test-types` passed.
- Cleanup checks found no residual fake MCP processes.

## Evidence

Attach at least one:

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [x] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

## Human Verification (required)

What you personally verified (not just CI), and how:

- Verified scenarios: direct SDK smoke for pending `tools/list`, direct SDK timeout smoke with request options, corrected RED behavior before the fix, source-runtime after-fix smoke, focused regression test, related MCP runtime tests, and `pnpm check:test-types`.
- Edge cases checked: per-server degrade/empty-catalog behavior, SDK cancellation after timeout, runtime disposal path, and fake server cleanup.
- What you did **not** verify: live third-party MCP servers, non-stdio MCP transports, malformed pagination/page limits, and full repository test suite.

## Review Conversations

- [ ] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

No bot review conversations exist for this new PR yet.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Config/env changes? (`Yes/No`) No
- Migration needed? (`Yes/No`) No
- If yes, exact upgrade steps: N/A

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: a very slow but eventually responsive MCP server may now be skipped during catalog discovery if it exceeds its configured `connectionTimeoutMs` during `tools/list`.
  - Mitigation: the timeout reuses the existing per-server connection budget, and failed servers already degrade per-server instead of rejecting the whole catalog.
- Risk: paginated `tools/list` responses can spend the timeout budget once per page.
  - Mitigation: this matches the SDK request-timeout shape for each page; malformed pagination/page-limit handling is intentionally out of scope for this narrow PR.

target.title: Policy: add gateway exposure checks
target.body:
## Summary

Adds a Policy 1.0 Gateway exposure conformance category with config-only evidence and doctor/lint findings.

Logical base: #81974 at signed head `d72baa8115`. This PR intentionally does not include runtime audit, gateway protocol, approval metadata, Swift protocol, or before-tool-call changes.

- Records Gateway bind/auth/rate-limit/Control UI/Tailscale/remote/HTTP posture as policy evidence only when Gateway policy rules are configured.
- Enforces non-loopback bind, disabled auth, missing explicit rate-limit posture, insecure Control UI toggles, Tailscale Funnel, remote mode, HTTP endpoints, and unrestricted URL-fetch posture.
- Treats blank, invalid, wildcard, or runtime-unrestricted values as non-conforming where the corresponding policy rule requires a constrained setting.
- Documents the new Gateway policy rules and evidence shape.

## Policy syntax

```jsonc
{
  "gateway": {
    "exposure": {
      "allowNonLoopbackBind": false,
      "allowTailscaleFunnel": false,
    },
    "auth": {
      "requireAuth": true,
      "requireExplicitRateLimit": true,
    },
    "controlUi": {
      "allowInsecure": false,
    },
    "remote": {
      "allow": false,
    },
    "http": {
      "denyEndpoints": ["chatCompletions", "responses"],
      "requireUrlAllowlists": true,
    },
  },
}
```

## Safety

This is config-level conformance only. `doctor --lint` and `policy check` read existing OpenClaw config and report findings. This PR does not launch a Gateway, mutate Gateway config, change Gateway runtime behavior, or add runtime enforcement.

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: adds policy conformance evidence and findings for Gateway exposure settings, including unsafe remote binding, disabled auth, missing rate-limit posture, insecure Control UI toggles, Tailscale Funnel, remote gateway mode, enabled HTTP endpoints, and unrestricted URL-fetch posture.

Real environment tested: WSL checkout at `/root/src/openclaw-81981-policy` on Ubuntu-24.04 after rebase onto #81974 head `d72baa8115`; PR head `494972ef4f` is signed with SSH key `SHA256:HBuDOOgXYqZ/EIBXkARPeFEEAs2IT+xOlS7y6p+chLc`.

Exact steps or command run after this patch:

- `pnpm exec oxfmt extensions/policy/src/policy-state.ts extensions/policy/src/doctor/register.test.ts`
- `git diff --check && git diff --cached --check`
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/policy/src/doctor/register.test.ts extensions/policy/src/cli.test.ts --run --reporter=dot`
- `node node_modules/@typescript/native-preview/bin/tsgo.js -p test/tsconfig/tsconfig.extensions.test.json --incremental --tsBuildInfoFile .artifacts/tsgo-cache/extensions-test-policy-81981.tsbuildinfo`
- `pnpm run lint:extensions:bundled`
- `pnpm docs:check-mdx docs/cli/policy.md docs/plugins/reference/policy.md`
- `/mnt/c/src/claws-hapi/.agents/skills/autoreview/scripts/autoreview --mode local`

Evidence after fix: focused policy doctor/CLI tests passed 2 files and 117 tests. Extension tsgo, bundled extension lint, docs MDX, diff whitespace checks, and autoreview passed. Autoreview accepted and fixed one finding for wildcard URL allowlists before the final clean pass.

Observed result after fix: Gateway policy evidence and findings now cover the configured conformance rules without adding runtime behavior. Wildcard URL allowlists such as `"*"` and `"*."` are reported as unrestricted when `gateway.http.requireUrlAllowlists` is true.

What was not tested: no live Gateway server was launched. The proof verifies config-level policy doctor/lint behavior against config and policy inputs.

## Related

- #80783 - Policy: add model, network, and MCP conformance checks
- #81974 - Policy: add secret and auth conformance checks

target.title: feat(tts): add skipEmojiSymbols option to prevent TTS from reading emoji/symbols
target.body:
## Summary

- Adds the opt-in `messages.tts.skipEmojiSymbols` TTS preprocessing setting.
- Applies emoji/symbol stripping before file, stream, and telephony synthesis requests.
- Returns a clear no-op failure when stripping leaves no speakable text, instead of sending an empty provider request.
- Keeps default behavior unchanged when the option is disabled or unset.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [x] Refactor required for the fix
- [x] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [ ] Gateway / orchestration
- [x] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [x] Integrations
- [x] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Refs #77969
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Motivation

TTS can turn emoji and decorative symbols into noisy spoken names. Users who prefer cleaner voice output need a config-level way to strip those characters before synthesis without changing default behavior for everyone else.

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior or issue addressed: optional stripping of emoji/symbol characters before TTS synthesis across file, stream, and telephony paths; empty-after-strip input is blocked before provider calls.
- Real environment tested: local OpenClaw checkout on Linux, branch `tts-skip-v3`, commit `06545b841d08`, using the bundled real Microsoft speech provider path (`extensions/microsoft` -> `node-edge-tts` -> `speech.platform.bing.com`). No API keys or private account secrets were used.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch:
  - `node --import tsx - <<'TS' ... register microsoft plugin, call textToSpeech twice with skipEmojiSymbols=false/true, then call an emoji-only enabled case ... TS`
  - `./node_modules/.bin/vitest run extensions/speech-core/src/tts.test.ts`
  - `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.agents-tools.config.ts src/agents/tools/tts-tool.test.ts`
  - `pnpm check:test-types && pnpm tsgo:prod`
- After-fix evidence:
  ```text
  $ node --import tsx - <<'TS'
  # Registers the bundled Microsoft Speech plugin and performs real synthesis
  # through node-edge-tts / speech.platform.bing.com after this patch.
  TS
  {
    "branch": "tts-skip-v3",
    "commit": "06545b841d08",
    "provider": "microsoft",
    "realProviderPath": "extensions/microsoft -> node-edge-tts -> speech.platform.bing.com",
    "inputText": "Proof phrase: package ready. Check mark ✓ copyright © melting face 🫠 done.",
    "observed": [
      {
        "name": "disabled-skipEmojiSymbols-false",
        "skipEmojiSymbols": false,
        "success": true,
        "provider": "microsoft",
        "outputFormat": "audio-24khz-48kbitrate-mono-mp3",
        "target": "audio-file",
        "savedArtifact": "/tmp/openclaw-78172-real-proof/disabled-skipEmojiSymbols-false.mp3",
        "bytes": 39456,
        "headerHex": "fff364c4"
      },
      {
        "name": "enabled-skipEmojiSymbols-true",
        "skipEmojiSymbols": true,
        "success": true,
        "provider": "microsoft",
        "outputFormat": "audio-24khz-48kbitrate-mono-mp3",
        "target": "audio-file",
        "savedArtifact": "/tmp/openclaw-78172-real-proof/enabled-skipEmojiSymbols-true.mp3",
        "bytes": 36288,
        "headerHex": "fff364c4"
      }
    ],
    "emptyAfterStrip": {
      "success": false,
      "error": "TTS text is empty after removing emoji and symbol characters",
      "provider": null
    },
    "artifactsDir": "/tmp/openclaw-78172-real-proof"
  }

  $ ffprobe -v error -show_entries format=duration,size ...
  ffprobe was not installed in this container, so artifact validation used byte size plus MP3 frame header checks.

  $ ./node_modules/.bin/vitest run extensions/speech-core/src/tts.test.ts
  Test Files  1 passed (1)
  Tests       32 passed (32)

  $ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.agents-tools.config.ts src/agents/tools/tts-tool.test.ts
  Test Files  1 passed (1)
  Tests       14 passed (14)

  $ pnpm check:test-types && pnpm tsgo:prod
  exit_code=0
  ```
- Observed result after fix: disabled mode generated real Microsoft speech audio from the original text containing `✓`, `©`, and `🫠`; enabled mode generated real Microsoft speech audio after the shared speech-core filter removed those symbol/emoji characters; emoji/symbol-only enabled input failed before any provider was selected.
- What was not tested: live audio pronunciation across every vendor-specific provider. The real-provider proof uses the bundled Microsoft speech provider because it is credential-free and exercises the same speech-core preprocessing path that all providers share.

## Root Cause (if applicable)

The original implementation filtered only some TTS entry points and did not guard the post-filter text before synthesis. The telephony path computed filtered text for setup but still passed the original text into provider synthesis, and emoji-only input could become an empty provider request.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level:
  - [ ] Unit test
  - [x] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file:
  - `extensions/speech-core/src/tts.test.ts`
  - `src/agents/tools/tts-tool.test.ts`
- Locked-in scenarios:
  - file synthesis strips complete emoji/symbol sequences when enabled.
  - stream synthesis does not call providers when stripping leaves empty text.
  - telephony synthesis receives stripped text when enabled.
  - telephony synthesis does not call providers when stripping leaves empty text.
  - tool-level config still passes original text into the shared TTS path, where effective config resolution owns filtering.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

Users can enable `messages.tts.skipEmojiSymbols` to strip emoji/symbol characters before speech synthesis. Unset or `false` preserves existing behavior.

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
text -> effective TTS config -> optional emoji/symbol strip -> empty guard -> provider synthesis
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`) No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`) No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- No security-impacting changes identified.

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: Linux
- Runtime/container: local repo checkout
- Model/provider: live proof speech-provider plugin plus focused Vitest coverage
- Integration/channel: speech-core file, stream, telephony, and TTS tool paths
- Relevant config: `messages.tts.skipEmojiSymbols=true|false`

### Steps

1. Run the live proof script shown above.
2. Run focused speech-core tests.
3. Run focused TTS tool tests.
4. Run test and production type gates.

### Expected

Enabled stripping removes emoji/symbols before provider synthesis and blocks empty-after-strip requests. Disabled mode preserves original text.

### Actual

The proof and tests match the expected behavior.

## Evidence

Included evidence:

- Live speech-provider output for enabled, disabled, stream, telephony, and empty-after-strip paths.
- Focused speech-core test output.
- Focused TTS tool test output.
- Test and production typecheck output.

## Human Verification (required)

- Verified scenarios: enabled/disabled filtering, file synthesis, stream synthesis, telephony synthesis, empty-after-strip guard, adjacent TTS tool behavior.
- Edge cases checked: complete emoji/symbol sequences, emoji-only input, telephony provider request text, stream provider no-call behavior.
- Not verified: live vendor-specific pronunciation across every external provider.

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Config/env changes? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Migration needed? (`Yes/No`) No
- Upgrade steps: optional new config `messages.tts.skipEmojiSymbols`; no change required when unset.

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: Unicode stripping removes characters a user wanted spoken.
- Mitigation: behavior is opt-in and disabled by default.
- Risk: filtering differs across TTS paths.
- Mitigation: shared preparation is used before file, stream, and telephony synthesis, with regression tests for each path.

target.title: fix(cli-runner): only the creating run closes the shared MCP loopback server
target.body:
## Summary

A run that **reused** an existing in-process MCP loopback server still tore it down on run end, killing a server another run was actively using.

`ensureMcpLoopbackServer()` returns a shared singleton. When a parent session created it and a one-shot child run (subagent, or `openclaw agent` child) reused it, the child still called `closeMcpLoopbackServer()` in its run-end `finally` because `cleanupBundleMcpOnRunEnd` was `true`. That closed the parent's still-live server. Subsequent loopback tool calls from the parent then failed with `fetch failed`, breaking agent-to-agent (A2A) bundle MCP tool use whenever a parent session and a one-shot child overlapped.

Repro signature: a parent agent session triggers a child agent run; after the child finishes, the parent's next bundle MCP tool call fails with `fetch failed` (observed as repeated consecutive failures across A2A invocations).

Regression introduced by `ccf2e77e8d` (*retire one-shot subagent MCP runtimes*), which routed child runs through the shared singleton + unconditional run-end close.

## Fix

Creator-responsible close:

- `ensureMcpLoopbackServer()` now returns `{ server, created }` — `created` is `true` only for the caller that actually started the server (or joined the in-flight start it triggered). Reusers get `created: false`.
- `prepare.ts` records this as `ownsBundleMcpLoopbackServer` on `PreparedCliRunContext`.
- `cli-runner.ts` closes the loopback server on run end only when `cleanupBundleMcpOnRunEnd === true && ownsBundleMcpLoopbackServer === true`. A run that merely reused a shared server never closes it.

Behavior matrix:

| caller | created/owns | cleanup flag | closes? |
|---|---|---|---|
| parent session creates | true | false (session) | no — server kept for reuse |
| one-shot child reuses | false | true (run) | **no — parent server survives (the fix)** |
| standalone CLI creates | true | true | yes |
| gateway shutdown | n/a | force path | yes (unconditional) |

The shutdown path (`server.impl.ts` → `closeMcpLoopbackServer()`) is unchanged and still tears the server down unconditionally.

## Verification

Local (this branch, off `main`):
- `pnpm test src/gateway/mcp-http.test.ts src/agents/cli-runner/prepare.test.ts` — 25 passed (covers the `created` flag for creator vs reuser, and re-create after full close).
- `pnpm build` — clean, no `[INEFFECTIVE_DYNAMIC_IMPORT]`.
- `pnpm check:changed` — typecheck (core) + core tests green, exit 0.

New regression test: `cli-runner.bundle-mcp.e2e.test.ts` › *"leaves a reused MCP loopback server running when a child run cleans up"* — pre-creates a loopback server (parent), runs a child with `cleanupBundleMcpOnRunEnd: true`, and asserts the runtime is still live afterwards. This fails without the owns-guard (child's unconditional close nulls the runtime).

> Note: the `cli-runner.bundle-mcp.e2e.test.ts` suite currently fails to load locally on `main` due to an unrelated pre-existing stale reference — `src/agents/auth-profiles/oauth.mirror-refresh.test.ts` mocks `@mariozechner/pi-ai/oauth`, but the package was rescoped to `@earendil-works/pi-ai`. That stale mock is not touched by this PR. The new e2e regression test passed on the prior base where the pi-ai scope still resolved. CI runs the e2e lane and will exercise it.

## Scope note

This is a behavior change in core run lifecycle (CODEOWNERS-sensitive). Kept minimal: the close decision is now gated on a prepared per-run ownership fact rather than the global cleanup flag alone. No new request-time discovery; the `created` fact is produced once in `prepare` and carried on the run context.

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)

target.title: Doctor: expose dry-run preview reports
target.body:
## Goal

Expose the structured Doctor repair work through top-level dry-run, diff, and JSON preview reporting.

The earlier PRs in this stack migrate repair buckets so they can return structured `changes`, `warnings`, `effects`, and `diffs`. This PR wires that output into the user-facing preview surface with `doctor --dry-run`, `--diff`, and JSON reporting, while explicitly reporting unconverted repair contributions as skipped instead of pretending every Doctor repair is ready.

Notes are threaded through like the rest of OpenClaw: presentation only through `ctx.doctor.note(...)`; preview JSON does not include `detect().notes`, `repair().notes`, runner note arrays, or note-derived findings.

## Legacy To Modern Shape

Before this stack, Doctor repair output was mostly interactive presentation and command-local behavior. The migrated repair checks now share an isomorphic shape:

- `detect()` produces the findings used by lint, repair, dry-run, and post-repair validation
- `repair()` returns structured repair output for the selected findings
- `doctor --dry-run` can preview repair actions without mutation
- `--diff` can show file-backed repair diffs where available
- JSON preview can report findings, changes, warnings, effects, diffs, and skipped unconverted repairs

This PR is intentionally honest about partial migration: the preview surface only exposes structured output for converted contributions and records skipped entries for legacy repairs that still need a follow-on conversion.

## Converted Here

- top-level `doctor --dry-run`
- top-level `doctor --diff`
- JSON preview reporting for structured repairs
- skipped records for unconverted repair contributions
- tests for dry-run/diff/JSON preview behavior across the migrated repair contract

## Review Surface

This is the preview-surface PR to challenge:

- whether `doctor --dry-run` should be enabled with the current migrated majority or remain gated longer
- whether skipped unconverted repairs are reported clearly enough for users and CI
- whether JSON fields for findings, changes, warnings, effects, and diffs are stable enough for consumers
- whether `--diff` should require dry-run or be valid in additional modes
- whether notes are correctly excluded from machine-readable preview output

## Stack Position

Parent: #84450 at `b1ee3977970e381966acb4b58f798a536ab881fd`. This is the final PR in the current Doctor repair stack.

The stack builds in order:

| PR | Area |
| --- | --- |
| #83753 | base interactive maintenance repairs and repair contract refinement |
| #84290 | plugin registry and configured plugin installs |
| #84326 | sandbox repairs |
| #84340 | gateway service repairs |
| #84366 | session state repairs |
| #84450 | config audit and cron repairs |
| #84472 | dry-run, diff, and JSON preview surface |

## Contract Examples In This PR

| Preview surface | User command | Reports | Contract behavior |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Top-level dry-run | `openclaw doctor --fix --dry-run` | findings, planned changes, warnings, effects, and skipped unconverted repair contributions | runs detection and structured repair planning without mutating state |
| Top-level diff preview | `openclaw doctor --fix --dry-run --diff` | all dry-run data plus file-backed diffs where converted repairs provide them | shows real textual diffs only for repairs that can describe file edits; non-file side effects remain `effects` |
| JSON preview | `openclaw doctor --fix --dry-run --json` | machine-readable findings, changes, warnings, effects, diffs, and skipped repairs | keeps notes out of JSON and reports unconverted contributions explicitly instead of silently pretending they are migrated |
| Guard for legacy repair slots | migrated plus unconverted doctor contribution mix | skipped records for repair contributions that do not yet expose structured repair output | allows the feature to be useful now while making the remaining conversion work visible and auditable |

This PR is the user-facing expression of the earlier contract tables: users can ask what `doctor --fix` would do, get concrete effects and real diffs before mutation, and see exactly which legacy repair slots still need conversion.

## Verification

- Restacked on #84450 as single commit `852500f81f5523dcdeff71d1795e3daabddb0277`.
- Manual review checked the preview guard path, JSON note suppression, unsupported-contribution skipped reporting, readonly gateway contribution behavior, and structured repair aggregation.
- `git diff --check` passed.
- `node node_modules\vitest\vitest.mjs run src/cli/program/register.maintenance.test.ts src/flows/doctor-health.test.ts src/flows/doctor-health-contributions.test.ts src/flows/doctor-repair-flow.test.ts src/flows/doctor-core-checks.test.ts` passed: 5 files, 112 tests.
- Nested `codex review --commit HEAD` was attempted, but its inner sandbox could not spawn PowerShell (`CreateProcessAsUserW failed: 1312`), so it did not provide meaningful findings.
- Fresh PR CI should provide broad proof across the full stack.

## Real Behavior Proof

Behavior addressed: `openclaw doctor` can expose structured dry-run, diff, and JSON preview output for migrated repair checks while reporting unconverted repair contributions as skipped.

Real environment tested: Windows worktree on the refreshed stack.

Exact steps or command run after this patch: `git diff --check`; `node node_modules\vitest\vitest.mjs run src/cli/program/register.maintenance.test.ts src/flows/doctor-health.test.ts src/flows/doctor-health-contributions.test.ts src/flows/doctor-repair-flow.test.ts src/flows/doctor-core-checks.test.ts`.

Evidence after fix: The focused test run passed 5 files and 112 tests, covering maintenance CLI registration, Doctor preview flow, JSON preview report generation, structured repair aggregation, and the shared repair flow/core check paths.

Observed result after fix: Converted repairs can be previewed through structured output, notes remain presentation-only, JSON preview is machine-readable, and unconverted repairs are reported as skipped instead of silently represented as migrated.

What was not tested: Full platform-wide Doctor CI and live repair scenarios were not rerun locally for this PR refresh.

target.title: agents: cap broad home find exec scans
target.body:
## Summary

When a request triggers a broad shell `find` over `$HOME` or `/` without an explicit tool timeout, the command can consume the entire embedded run budget and fall through to the generic 600s run timeout.

This change adds a narrow guard for that failure mode:

- cap broad home/root `find` scans at 20s when no explicit exec timeout is provided
- keep explicit operator intent intact when the command already sets `timeout`, `-prune`, `-xdev`, or `-mount`
- add system prompt guidance to prefer scoped workspace searches over broad shell scans

## Why

A recent timeout investigation showed an agent trying to locate a project directory by running a command equivalent to:

```bash
find $HOME -maxdepth 5 -type d -iname '*target*'
```

That search walked into noisy, permission-heavy trees and lasted until the embedded run hit its global 600s timeout. The user request itself was reasonable; the pathological part was letting an unbounded home-directory discovery command inherit the full run budget.

## Testing

- `pnpm test src/agents/bash-tools.exec.broad-find-guard.test.ts`
- `pnpm test src/agents/bash-tools.exec.script-preflight.test.ts`
- `pnpm test src/agents/system-prompt.test.ts -t "guides subagent workflows to avoid polling loops"`

target.title: feat(session): honor session.mainKey config for custom main session routing
target.body:
Fixes #41790

## Summary
Allow `session.mainKey` to designate any channel session as the canonical main session, instead of unconditionally overriding it to `"main"`.

## Changes
- **`src/config/defaults.ts`**: Preserve user-configured `mainKey` instead of unconditionally overriding to `"main"`. Remove now-unused warning machinery (`SessionDefaultsOptions`, `WarnState`, `resetSessionDefaultsWarningForTests`).
- **`src/routing/resolve-route.ts`**: Read `mainKey` from config via `normalizeMainKey()` instead of always using `DEFAULT_MAIN_KEY`.
- **`src/web/auto-reply/monitor/broadcast.ts`**: Same — use configured `mainKey` for broadcast route keys.
- **Tests**: Added unit tests for both `applySessionDefaults` (custom key preservation, normalization, trimming) and `resolveAgentRoute` (custom mainKey with default and per-channel-peer dmScope).

## Motivation
When using `dmScope: "per-channel-peer"`, the owner's DM session gets its own key (e.g., `agent:main:telegram:direct:123456`). Setting `session.mainKey` to this key allows heartbeats, cron, system events, and all main-session traffic to route directly to the owner's chat, while keeping other users' DMs isolated.

Currently, `applySessionDefaults()` unconditionally overrides `mainKey` to `"main"` and logs a warning. This PR removes that override and lets the configured value flow through to routing.

target.title: fix(models) Discord model picker doesn't list all models
target.body:
## Add pagination to Discord model picker


https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/de121ab3-bedd-4de7-b5ce-3334d95633ea



Previously the Discord `/models` picker only showed the first page of providers and the first 25 models per provider — Discord component limits cap the provider grid at 25 buttons and each select menu at 25 options. Users with >25 providers or any provider with >25 models (openai, anthropic, etc.) couldn't see the rest of the catalog.

Adds a `[◀ Prev | Page X/Y | Next ▶]` row to both views when `totalPages > 1`:
- Provider view: nav row appended below the button grid; footer shows page X/Y · N providers total.
- Model view: nav row inserted between the model select and the trailing button row; detail lines include a `provider: page X/Y · N models` line.

Page indicator is a disabled informational button; prev/next disable at endpoints. Custom_id encodes the target page plus the surrounding state (selected provider, runtime, pending model) so flipping pages preserves the picker's selection. Row budget worst case is 5 in both views, within `DISCORD_COMPONENT_MAX_ROWS`.

## Real behavior proof
Behavior addressed: Grok `web_search` can be selected during onboarding/config when xAI OAuth already exists, runtime prefers OAuth, retries refreshed OAuth on 401, and falls back to xAI API-key auth/profile/env/config when OAuth remains stale. The active session agent directory is now threaded into web_search provider selection and execution, so non-default agents use their own xAI OAuth/API-key auth profiles. xAI catalog now includes `grok-build-0.1`; xAI image/video provider defaults use a 600s operation timeout. The final onboarding summary now treats an existing xAI OAuth auth profile as a valid `web_search` credential instead of reporting a missing API key.
Real environment tested: Local macOS source checkout on branch `codex/xai-oauth-web-search`, Node/pnpm repo toolchain, plus an isolated onboarding proof state seeded with a fake xAI OAuth auth profile. No real credentials were used.
Exact steps or command run after this patch: Ran the Verification commands listed above, then ran `openclaw onboard --flow advanced --mode local --auth-choice skip --workspace <isolated-proof-workspace> --accept-risk --skip-daemon --skip-channels --skip-skills --skip-hooks --skip-health --skip-ui --skip-bootstrap --secret-input-mode plaintext` against isolated state with Grok selected for web_search.
Evidence after fix: Focused Vitest shards passed; SDK/config baseline checks passed; docs/format/boundary/build gates passed; final local and current-HEAD autoreview runs were clean. Onboarding screenshot proof is attached in https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/85182#issuecomment-4515250321, with sanitized proof bundle at https://gist.github.com/fuller-stack-dev/25f1005f74d30b467d6064acbd7b21c9.
Observed result after fix: OAuth-backed Grok web_search tests use xAI OAuth before configured API-key fallback, refresh on 401, fall back to a non-OAuth API-key auth profile when stale OAuth remains first, and pass active `agentDir` through generic web_search auto-detect and provider execution. Onboarding skips the API-key prompt when xAI OAuth exists and the final summary reports `Credential: existing xAI OAuth sign-in.`.
What was not tested: Live xAI OAuth/API calls were not run from the onboarding proof; behavior is covered with mocked provider-auth and HTTP responses plus local onboarding proof using a fake OAuth profile.

target.title: fix(agents): derive overflow budgets from provider errors
target.body:
## Summary

- Broaden observed overflow token extraction across provider-shaped error formats used during overflow recovery.
- Pass a minimally over-budget `currentTokenCount` into overflow compaction when overflow is confirmed but the provider message does not expose a parseable count.
- Add regression coverage and compaction docs for the shared overflow-budget path.
- Refresh the PR on current `origin/main` (`c8a35c4645dcf926d8e2c8165a1b0a2e48d24296`) and resolve the previous merge conflicts.

## Relevance check

Still relevant. Current `main` still classified Kimi/MiniMax/additive provider overflow messages as context overflow, but `extractObservedOverflowTokenCount()` only parsed older `prompt is too long: N tokens > ...`, `requested N tokens`, and `resulted in N tokens` forms. The runner still omitted `currentTokenCount` unless extraction succeeded, so confirmed-but-unparseable overflow could still fall through to insufficient budget compaction.

## Review comments addressed

- Removed the redundant `you requested ... tokens` regex from the old review path; it is not present in the refreshed branch.
- Refreshed the branch against current `main`, resolving the dirty/conflicting state.
- Updated the overflow diagnostic log to report both `observedTokens` and the effective `compactionTokens` so synthetic fallback counts are distinguishable from provider-reported counts.

## Verification

- Current head: `0701ef671160c05f730a31c9f4d07218c08f2894`
- Rebasing target: `upstream/main` at `cd1cae5be9ba76763e696784ff2effa534e00052`
- `pnpm docs:list`
- `git diff --check upstream/main...HEAD`
- `pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 docs/reference/session-management-compaction.md src/agents/pi-embedded-helpers.isbillingerrormessage.test.ts src/agents/pi-embedded-helpers/errors.ts src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/run.overflow-compaction.test.ts src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/run.ts`
- Focused Vitest through `scripts/run-vitest.mjs` for `src/agents/pi-embedded-helpers.isbillingerrormessage.test.ts` and `src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/run.overflow-compaction.test.ts`
  - Result: `Test Files 3 passed (3)`, `Tests 201 passed (201)`.
- `AUTOREVIEW_AUTO_TESTS=0 .agents/skills/autoreview/scripts/autoreview --mode branch --base upstream/main`
  - Result: `autoreview clean: no accepted/actionable findings reported`.

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: Provider-shaped context-overflow errors that already trigger overflow recovery now provide an effective overflow budget to compaction. Parseable provider counts are forwarded as `currentTokenCount`; confirmed overflow with no parseable count uses `ctxInfo.tokens + 1` so budget-targeted compaction has an over-budget count.

Real environment tested: Real local OpenClaw source checkout after rebasing PR #70473 onto `upstream/main` `cd1cae5be9ba76763e696784ff2effa534e00052`. The terminal probe loaded the exported OpenClaw overflow classifier/parser from `src/agents/pi-embedded-helpers.ts` and applied the same fallback budget calculation used by the embedded runner before compaction.

Exact steps or command run after this patch: Ran this terminal probe after the rebase and review fixes:

```sh
pnpm exec tsx --eval 'import { extractObservedOverflowTokenCount, isContextOverflowError } from "./src/agents/pi-embedded-helpers.ts"; /* probe provider overflow samples and ctxInfo.tokens + 1 fallback */'
```

Evidence after fix: Terminal output from the local OpenClaw checkout showed parseable provider counts being carried through and confirmed-but-unparseable overflow messages receiving the synthetic over-budget count:

```text
minimax: overflow=true observed=203557 compactionTokens=203557
kimi: overflow=true observed=291351 compactionTokens=291351
additive: overflow=true observed=204705 compactionTokens=204705
unparseable: overflow=true observed=none compactionTokens=200001
characters: overflow=true observed=none compactionTokens=200001
```

Observed result after fix: The refreshed branch is mergeable on GitHub, and the overflow-budget probe shows the after-fix behavior: MiniMax/Kimi/additive provider messages yield their observed token counts, while confirmed overflow without a parseable token count yields `ctxInfo.tokens + 1` (`200001` in the probe) for compaction.

What was not tested: A fresh live provider overflow against MiniMax/Kimi/opencode-go was not rerun, and the full repository build/check suite was not run locally. GitHub CI is running on the refreshed head.

## Notes

- Related to #70472, but does not fully resolve later session-rotation/orphaning behavior after failed compaction.

target.title: feat(zalouser): normalize agent-emitted markdown for Zalo client rendering
target.body:
## Summary

The Zalo personal-account client (mobile + web) renders agent-style markdown poorly in two specific ways that the zalouser plugin currently passes through unchanged:

1. **Triple-dash / triple-asterisk horizontal rules** (`---`, `***`) render as literal dashes / asterisks on their own line - never as a separator. LLM agents emit them as section dividers; recipients see them as garbage.

2. **List items separated by blank lines** render with the blank line preserved, breaking the visual grouping a reader expects from a markdown list. `- foo\n- bar` reads as one list; `- foo\n\n- bar` reads as two unrelated items with extra vertical space. LLM agents commonly emit lists this way because Slack / Discord / the web playground render that style best - Zalo is the outlier.

This PR adds a `normalizeZalouserOutboundText` pre-processor invoked at the top of `sendMessageZalouser`. Block-aware (skips fenced code blocks), idempotent (only removes characters, never adds), style-safe (skips normalization when caller-supplied plain-mode textStyles are present, since the transport layer does not rebase those offsets).

## Tests

**`outbound-format.test.ts`** - 25 cases: identity / passthrough, HR stripping positive + negative, inter-item blank-line collapse for bullet / asterisk / numbered, edge cases at list boundaries, hard newline cap, fenced code block preservation (triple-backtick + triple-tilde, longer fences, lists-inside-fence, prose-around-fence, unclosed fence), idempotency property.

**`send.test.ts`** - 3 new integration cases: end-to-end normalization on list-with-blanks, fence preservation through `sendMessageZalouser`, plain-mode skip with caller-supplied textStyles.

Full zalouser package suite: 185 passed, 2 skipped (unrelated).

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior or issue addressed**: Inbound LLM-generated markdown containing a bullet list with blank lines between items rendered on the Zalo personal-account client as visually fragmented paragraphs (one blank line of vertical space between each bullet), instead of as a tight grouped list. Same for `---` horizontal-rule lines - they rendered as literal dashes instead of as a separator.

**Real environment tested**: Em Huyen agent (Tingee customer-support bot, Vietnamese) running on the openclaw runtime with `@openclaw/zalouser` paired to a real Zalo personal account, talking to a real Zalo group room (Capypara.AI). Tested on both Zalo Mobile (Android client) and Zalo Desktop (dark theme) on the same conversation, after `normalizeZalouserOutboundText` was wired into `sendMessageZalouser`.

**Exact steps or command run after this patch**:
1. Apply this PR's `outbound-format.ts` + `send.ts` changes to the running zalouser plugin.
2. Restart the agent container so the plugin reloads.
3. From a separate Zalo account, send a photo of a Tingee soundbox label to the Capypara.AI group with the text `@Em Huyền reset loa cho anh`.
4. Wait for Em Huyen to reply (the bot uses markdown with `- ...` bullets separated by blank lines for its hotline / Zalo OA list).
5. Open the same conversation on Zalo Android and on Zalo Desktop; screenshot the bot's reply on each.

**Evidence after fix**:

Zalo Android (Capypara.AI group, dark theme):

![Em Huyen reply on Zalo Android](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/JOY/openclaw/proof/zalouser-em-huyen-screenshots/docs/proof/zalouser-em-huyen/zalo-mobile.jpg)

Zalo Desktop (same conversation):

![Em Huyen reply on Zalo Desktop](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/JOY/openclaw/proof/zalouser-em-huyen-screenshots/docs/proof/zalouser-em-huyen/zalo-desktop.jpg)

Both screenshots show the reply with two bullets (`📞 Hotline: 1900 255 567` + `💬 Zalo OA: Tingee by Heno`) rendered as a tight group with NO blank line between them. The agent's raw output emitted blank lines between each bullet - the post-fix normalizer collapses them before they reach the Zalo client.

**Observed result after fix**: On both Zalo Mobile and Zalo Desktop, the two-bullet list renders as one tight visual group. Recipients no longer see contact options fragmented into separate paragraphs. `---` HR lines from the same agent are also no longer emitted as literal dashes (covered in the same normalizer pass).

**What was not tested**: I have not run a head-to-head BEFORE / AFTER screenshot pair in the same Zalo conversation - the pre-fix rendering was the live deployment state captured separately in a downstream vendor patch's tracking ticket, which is what motivated this PR. The AFTER screenshots above are from a real deployment with the fix in place. Maintainers with a Zalo test account can reproduce the BEFORE state by reverting `extensions/zalouser/src/send.ts` to the pre-PR version and re-sending the same agent reply.

Screenshots live on a separate `proof/zalouser-em-huyen-screenshots` branch on the fork so the upstream merge does not import binaries into openclaw history.

## Refs

Companion to openclaw/openclaw#84924 (inbound photo support upstream PR). Both came from the same deployment surfacing real rendering gaps in the zalouser plugin.

target.title: Doctor: structure config audit and cron repairs
target.body:
## Goal

Move config audit scrub and legacy cron repairs into the structured Doctor repair contract.

This wave covers repairs where diffs matter most: config cleanup, audit scrub behavior, and legacy cron migration. Existing repair policy stays intact, while dry-run and repair output now expose concrete `changes`, `warnings`, `effects`, and `diffs` where the repair can describe file edits.

Notes are threaded through like the rest of OpenClaw: presentation only through `ctx.doctor.note(...)`; no `detect().notes`, no `repair().notes`, no runner note arrays.

## Legacy To Modern Shape

Config audit and cron repairs used to be command-local repair flows with presentation mixed into the mutation path. This PR keeps the same repair decisions while making the output isomorphic:

- `detect()` reports config audit and cron findings without mutation
- `repair()` receives the selected findings and performs only those repairs
- dry-run reports what would be scrubbed, migrated, or rewritten
- diffs are emitted where file edits can be represented usefully
- repair effects and warnings give preview/reporting surfaces stable machine signal

## Converted Here

- config audit scrub repairs
- legacy cron repairs
- cron/config repair effects
- diffs where available for file-backed repair output
- focused tests around config/cron repair behavior and shared repair-flow behavior

## Review Surface

This is the config/cron-policy PR to challenge:

- whether config audit scrub findings are grouped at the right level for repair and preview
- whether cron migration effects should name legacy entries, target schedules, or files differently
- whether the emitted diffs are the right level of detail for `doctor --dry-run --diff`
- whether warnings represent real operator decisions rather than presentation text

## Stack Position

Parent: #84366 at `e75bfa2708f0999c52cfa51320df7c07ff986ee5`. This PR is the config audit and cron wave in the Doctor repair stack.

The follow-on PR exposes the top-level dry-run/diff preview surface once enough repair buckets have structured output.

## Contract Examples In This PR

| Repair area | Detects | Repairs | Dry-run / diff / effect proof |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Config audit scrub | config audit state that should be scrubbed or normalized | applies the existing audit scrub repair policy | `doctor --fix --dry-run --diff` shows planned file-backed scrub edits as diffs where possible, with effects/warnings for the operator-visible action |
| Legacy cron migration | legacy cron entries or schedules that need the modern OpenClaw representation | runs the existing cron normalization/migration repair | dry-run reports the cron target and planned migration before rewriting; `--diff` is used for concrete file/schedule text changes where available |
| Cron/config warnings | repair conditions that still require operator awareness | keeps warnings in structured repair output | warnings stay `warnings`, not presentation notes, so JSON/dry-run consumers can report them consistently |

This is the first stack slice where textual diffs matter most. The contract is that `--diff` should show concrete file-backed edits when the repair can describe them, while effects explain the broader cron/config action and dry-run prevents mutation.

## Verification

- `git diff --check`
- `node node_modules\vitest\vitest.mjs run src/flows/doctor-core-checks.test.ts src/flows/doctor-health-contributions.test.ts src/commands/doctor-config-audit-scrub.test.ts src/commands/doctor-cron.test.ts`
- Fresh PR CI should provide broad proof across the full stack.

## Real Behavior Proof

Behavior addressed: Config audit and legacy cron repairs now use structured findings, scoped repair, dry-run output, repair effects, and diffs where available while preserving existing repair policy.

Real environment tested: Windows isolated worktree at commit `b1ee3977970e381966acb4b58f798a536ab881fd`, restacked on #84366.

Exact steps or command run after this patch: `node node_modules\vitest\vitest.mjs run src/flows/doctor-core-checks.test.ts src/flows/doctor-health-contributions.test.ts src/commands/doctor-config-audit-scrub.test.ts src/commands/doctor-cron.test.ts`.

Evidence after fix: 3 focused config/cron/Doctor test files passed, 86 tests total, covering config audit scrub detection/repair, legacy cron normalization, dry-run file effects, positional contribution ordering, and detect-after validation.

Observed result after fix: Config audit and cron repair behavior remains available through Doctor, while dry-run and later preview surfaces can inspect planned file edits before mutation. Notes stay presentation-only.

What was not tested: Live cron migration scenarios and platform-wide Doctor CI were not rerun locally for this body refresh.
